section.title: The Priestly Garments Completed
section.sectionRationale: The craftsmen finish the priestly garments exactly as commanded, the refrain 'just as the LORD had commanded Moses' repeating throughout, and every piece of the tabernacle is brought to Moses, who inspects it all and blesses the people for their faithful obedience.
section.thesis: The skilled workers complete the ephod, breastpiece, robe, and remaining priestly garments precisely as the LORD commanded, and when everything is brought to Moses, he inspects it all and blesses the people because they had done exactly as the LORD instructed.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the ephod and its memorial stones; the breastpiece of judgment with its gemstones; the robe and remaining garments; and everything brought to Moses, inspected, and found to match every command.

units.label: The ephod
units.note: just as the LORD had commanded Moses

media.alt: Finished priestly garments laid before a tent
media.caption: Every garment is finished exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.

verses.text: From the blue, purple, and scarlet yarn they made specially woven garments for ministry in the sanctuary, as well as the holy garments for Aaron, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
Bezalel made the ephod of finely spun linen embroidered with gold, and with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn.
They hammered out thin sheets of gold and cut threads from them to interweave with the blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and fine linen, the work of a skilled craftsman.
They made shoulder pieces for the ephod, which were attached at two of its corners, so it could be fastened.

And the skillfully woven waistband of the ephod was of one piece with the ephod, of the same workmanship, with gold, with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and with finely spun linen, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
They mounted the onyx stones in gold filigree settings, engraved like a seal with the names of the sons of Israel.
Then they fastened them on the shoulder pieces of the ephod as memorial stones for the sons of Israel, as the LORD had commanded Moses.
He made the breastpiece with the same workmanship as the ephod, with gold, with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and with finely spun linen.
It was square when folded over double, a span long and a span wide.
And they mounted on it four rows of gemstones: The first row had a ruby, a topaz, and an emerald;
the second row had a turquoise, a sapphire, and a diamond;
the third row had a jacinth, an agate, and an amethyst;
and the fourth row had a beryl, an onyx, and a jasper. These stones were mounted in gold filigree settings.
The twelve stones corresponded to the names of the sons of Israel. Each stone was engraved like a seal with the name of one of the twelve tribes.
For the breastpiece they made braided chains like cords of pure gold.
They also made two gold filigree settings and two gold rings, and fastened the two rings to the two corners of the breastpiece.
Then they fastened the two gold chains to the two gold rings at the corners of the breastpiece,
and they fastened the other ends of the two chains to the two filigree settings, attaching them to the shoulder pieces of the ephod at the front.
They made two more gold rings and attached them to the other two corners of the breastpiece, on the inside edge next to the ephod.
They made two additional gold rings and attached them to the bottom of the two shoulder pieces of the ephod, on its front, near the seam just above its woven waistband.

Then they tied the rings of the breastpiece to the rings of the ephod with a cord of blue yarn, so that the breastpiece was above the waistband of the ephod and would not swing out from the ephod, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.

units.label: The robe and remaining garments
units.note: just as the LORD had commanded Moses

verses.text: They made the robe of the ephod entirely of blue cloth, the work of a weaver,
with an opening in the center of the robe like that of a garment, with a collar around the opening so that it would not tear.
They made pomegranates of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and finely spun linen on the lower hem of the robe.
They also made bells of pure gold and attached them around the hem between the pomegranates,
alternating the bells and pomegranates around the lower hem of the robe to be worn for ministry, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
For Aaron and his sons they made tunics of fine linen, the work of a weaver,
as well as the turban of fine linen, the ornate headbands and undergarments of finely spun linen,
and the sash of finely spun linen, embroidered with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
They also made the plate of the holy crown of pure gold, and they engraved on it, like an inscription on a seal: {{holy|HOLY TO THE LORD}}.
Then they fastened to it a blue cord to mount it on the turban, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.

units.label: Brought to Moses, and blessed
units.note: just as the LORD had commanded

media.alt: A leader inspecting finished tabernacle items
media.caption: Moses inspects all the finished work and blesses the people.

verses.text: So all the work for the tabernacle, the Tent of Meeting, was completed. The Israelites did everything just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
Then they brought the tabernacle to Moses: the tent with all its furnishings, its clasps, its frames, its crossbars, and its posts and bases;
the covering of ram skins dyed red, the covering of fine leather, and the veil of the covering;
the ark of the Testimony with its poles and the mercy seat;
the table with all its utensils and the Bread of the Presence;
the pure gold lampstand with its row of lamps and all its utensils, as well as the oil for the light;
the gold altar, the anointing oil, the fragrant incense, and the curtain for the entrance to the tent;
the bronze altar with its bronze grating, its poles, and all its utensils; the basin with its stand;

the curtains of the courtyard with its posts and bases; the curtain for the gate of the courtyard, its ropes and tent pegs, and all the equipment for the service of the tabernacle, the Tent of Meeting;
and the woven garments for ministering in the sanctuary, both the holy garments for Aaron the priest and the garments for his sons to serve as priests.
The Israelites had done all the work just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
And Moses inspected all the work and saw that they had accomplished it just as the LORD had commanded. So Moses {{blessed|blessed}} them.

holy.gloss: Holy to the LORD, engraved on the gold plate worn by Aaron. First commanded in chapter 28, it is here completed exactly as instructed, marking the entire finished project's ultimate purpose: everything set apart wholly for God.

blessed.gloss: To bless, to speak good and favor over someone. Moses' blessing here, after inspecting the completed work, echoes the pattern of blessing that follows faithful completion of God's commands throughout Scripture, most notably at creation itself.

basic.context: The craftsmen finish every priestly garment, ephod, breastpiece, robe, and remaining pieces, exactly as God had commanded, with the phrase 'just as the LORD had commanded Moses' repeated again and again. Everything is then brought to Moses, who personally inspects the entire completed tabernacle and blesses the people for their faithful obedience.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a historical record of exact, careful obedience to earlier divine instruction, not a text requiring symbolic decoding of every detail. Its central theological weight lies in the repeated refrain of obedience and in Moses' concluding inspection and blessing, echoing patterns of completed work and divine approval found elsewhere in Scripture.
basic.originalLanguages: The refrain ka'asher tzivah Yahweh et Mosheh, just as the LORD had commanded Moses, appears repeatedly throughout the chapter, emphasizing precise fidelity to instruction. The gold plate again reads qodesh la-Yahweh, holy to the LORD, and Moses' final act is to barak, bless, the people for their faithful work.
basic.typology: The pattern of completed work followed by inspection and blessing echoes the seventh day of creation, when God saw all that He had made and blessed it. This anticipates the completed work of Christ, who declares 'It is finished' before God's approval is shown in the resurrection, and the pattern of faithful stewardship rewarded that runs throughout Scripture.
basic.christ: Just as Moses inspects the completed tabernacle and finds it done exactly as commanded, God the Father vindicates Christ's finished work of redemption through the resurrection, the ultimate 'well done' spoken over perfectly completed obedience, Hebrews 10:5-10. The repeated phrase of exact obedience here anticipates Christ's own perfect fulfillment of everything the Father commanded.
basic.god: God's detailed commands are met with detailed, faithful obedience, and He responds, through Moses, with blessing, echoing the pattern of creation itself: work completed as commanded leads to divine approval and blessing. God delights not merely in good intentions but in the actual, careful completion of what He has asked.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 1:31-2:3 records God seeing all He had made, declaring it very good, and blessing the seventh day, the pattern this chapter echoes. John 19:30 records Christ's own completed work, 'It is finished.' Matthew 25:21 speaks of the master's 'well done, good and faithful servant.'
basic.oneStory: Israel completes every part of the tabernacle exactly as God commanded, and Moses inspects and blesses the finished work, echoing the pattern of creation completed and blessed. This pattern of faithful completion followed by divine approval reaches its fullest expression in Christ, whose finished work of redemption receives the Father's ultimate approval in the resurrection.

context.literary: This chapter closes the tabernacle construction narrative with a deliberate rhythm, each section ending in the refrain 'just as the LORD had commanded Moses,' before a final itemized list of everything brought to Moses and his personal inspection and blessing, forming a fitting climax to the entire building account begun in chapter 25.
context.historical: The repeated refrain of exact obedience stands as a deliberate contrast to Israel's earlier disobedience with the golden calf, showing a genuinely transformed, careful faithfulness to God's revealed will in this second attempt at building what God had originally intended.
context.canonical: The pattern here, work completed, inspected, and blessed, echoes Genesis 1 and 2's creation account so closely that many scholars see the tabernacle deliberately presented as a new creation, a microcosm where God's original purposes for dwelling with His people are being restored.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the exact, faithful completion of every priestly garment and tabernacle furnishing according to God's commands, and Moses' resulting blessing, showing that careful obedience to God's revealed will results in His approval and favor.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a historical narrative describing the specific completion of Israel's tabernacle construction; it does not prescribe a ritual inspection process for churches today. Its abiding truth is the blessing that follows faithful, careful obedience to what God has commanded.

debates.question: Is the parallel between this chapter's completion and blessing and Genesis 1's creation and blessing an intentional literary device by the author, or a coincidental similarity?

views.label: A deliberate, intentional parallel
views.case: Many scholars argue the repeated structure, seven speeches in the instructions, seven-fold repetition of completion, and the concluding blessing, deliberately echoes Genesis 1 to present the tabernacle as a renewed creation, where God's presence dwells among His people as in Eden.

views.label: A natural, non-deliberate similarity of completion narratives
views.case: Others are more cautious about pressing exact numerical or structural parallels, noting that any account of a great work finished and approved would naturally share some of this same shape, without necessarily implying the author consciously modeled it after the creation account.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm the same substantial theological point: careful, faithful completion of God's revealed will results in blessing, whether or not the precise literary parallel to Genesis 1 was fully intentional.

study.originalLanguages: The refrain ka'asher tzivah Yahweh et Mosheh, just as the LORD had commanded Moses, punctuates the chapter repeatedly, underscoring exact fidelity to divine instruction rather than mere approximate effort. The chapter closes with Moses' barak, blessing, of the people, echoing the same verb used of God's blessing over completed creation in Genesis 1 and 2.

typology.type: Completed work inspected and blessed, echoing creation's seventh day
typology.fulfillment: God's approval of Christ's finished work of redemption, vindicated in the resurrection, Romans 1:4.

typology.type: The repeated refrain of exact obedience to every command
typology.fulfillment: Christ's perfect, complete fulfillment of everything the Father commanded Him, John 17:4.

study.christ: Just as Moses inspects the tabernacle and finds it completed exactly as the LORD commanded, resulting in blessing, Jesus declares of His own redemptive work, 'It is finished,' John 19:30, and God the Father vindicates that finished work by raising Him from the dead, Romans 1:4, the ultimate divine approval of perfectly completed obedience.
study.god: God's detailed commands, given across seven chapters of instruction, are met here with equally detailed, faithful obedience, and God, through Moses, responds with blessing, mirroring His own pattern at creation of seeing completed work as good and blessing it. This shows that God delights in careful, faithful completion of what He commands, not merely good intentions.

crossReferences.note: God sees all He has made, declares it very good, and blesses the completed work, the pattern this chapter echoes.
Christ declares His redemptive work finished, echoing this pattern of completed obedience.
Christ's finished work is vindicated and declared through His resurrection.

study.oneStory: Israel completes every detail of the tabernacle exactly as commanded, and Moses inspects and blesses the finished work, echoing God's own blessing over completed creation. This pattern, faithful completion met with divine approval, culminates in Christ, whose finished work of redemption receives the Father's fullest vindication in the resurrection.

quiz.q: What phrase is repeated throughout this chapter to describe how the priestly garments were made?

options.text: Just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
options.note: This exact refrain appears repeatedly, including in verses 1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, and 31.

options.text: According to the wisdom of the elders.
options.note: The repeated refrain specifically credits the LORD's command through Moses, not the elders' independent wisdom.

options.text: As Bezalel personally preferred.
options.note: The emphasis throughout is on obedience to God's command, not Bezalel's personal preference.

options.text: No refrain is repeated in this chapter.
options.note: The phrase just as the LORD had commanded Moses is explicitly repeated multiple times.

quiz.q: What did the twelve stones on the breastpiece represent, according to verse 14?

options.text: The names of the twelve tribes of Israel, one stone per tribe.
options.note: Verse 14 states this correspondence explicitly.

options.text: The twelve months of the Hebrew calendar.
options.note: The stones correspond to the twelve tribes, not a calendar system.

options.text: The twelve plagues brought upon Egypt.
options.note: There were ten plagues, not twelve, and the stones represent the tribes, not the plagues.

options.text: No meaning is given for the twelve stones.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly explains the stones represent the twelve tribes.

quiz.q: What was engraved on the gold plate of the holy crown, according to verse 30?

options.text: HOLY TO THE LORD.
options.note: Verse 30 gives this exact inscription, matching the original command in chapter 28.

options.text: The names of Moses and Aaron.
options.note: The inscription is a declaration of holiness to the LORD, not the names of Moses and Aaron.

options.text: The Ten Commandments in full.
options.note: The commandments were on the stone tablets, not engraved on this gold plate.

options.text: No inscription is mentioned.
options.note: Verse 30 explicitly gives the inscription: Holy to the LORD.

quiz.q: What did Moses do after inspecting all the completed work, according to verse 43?

options.text: He blessed the people.
options.note: Verse 43 states this exact response after his inspection.

options.text: He ordered the work redone from scratch.
options.note: The text states the work matched every command exactly, so nothing needed to be redone; Moses blessed the people instead.

options.text: He remained silent and said nothing.
options.note: Verse 43 explicitly records Moses' blessing, not silence.

options.text: He rebuked the people for delays.
options.note: The text records approval and blessing, not rebuke, since the work matched every command.

quiz.q: What conclusion does verse 43 draw about how the Israelites had completed the work?

options.text: They had accomplished it just as the LORD had commanded.
options.note: Verse 43 states this exact conclusion, prompting Moses' blessing.

options.text: They had completed only half of what was required.
options.note: The verse specifically affirms complete, exact fulfillment of every command, not a partial completion.

options.text: They had substituted their own preferred designs.
options.note: The verse emphasizes exact conformity to the LORD's command, not personal substitution or preference.

options.text: No evaluation of the work is given.
options.note: Verse 43 explicitly evaluates the work as accomplished just as the LORD commanded.

questions: The repeated phrase 'just as the LORD had commanded Moses' marks every stage of this work. Where in your own obedience do you tend to settle for approximate faithfulness rather than careful, exact obedience to what God has asked?

Moses personally inspected every piece before blessing the people, taking the time to verify careful completion. How do you respond when your own work or service is closely examined, with defensiveness or openness?

The blessing came only after the work matched God's command exactly, echoing the pattern of creation completed and blessed. Where do you look for approval, from finishing well according to God's standard, or from other measures of success?

The gold plate reading 'Holy to the LORD' had been commanded back in chapter 28 and is only now, after much patient work, actually completed and worn. What long-obeyed but not yet finished commitment in your own life needs patient follow-through to completion?

This whole massive project, after the disaster of the golden calf, ends in blessing rather than further judgment. Where do you need hope that faithful obedience today can be part of God restoring what was broken by past failure?

translationNotes.note: The phrase just as the LORD had commanded Moses recurs so consistently through this chapter, appearing at least seven times, that many scholars see it as a deliberate structural echo of the sevenfold pattern of divine speeches and completion language in Genesis 1.

The tabernacle, the Tent of Meeting was completed uses both names for the structure together, emphasizing that the physical building work, and the purpose of meeting with God it was built for, were both now fully realized.

Moses blessed them echoes the same verb, barak, used of God blessing the completed work of creation in Genesis 2:3, reinforcing the tabernacle's presentation as a kind of new creation where God will dwell among His people.
